title: Friar Tuck
summary: Of all Robin Hood's Merry Men, none was more surprising than the holy man who could fight like a soldier and laugh like a drunkard.
full_text:
Of all Robin Hood's Merry Men, none was more surprising than the holy man who could fight like a soldier and laugh like a drunkard. His name was Friar Tuck, and he was a curtal friar, a wandering monk who had left the walls of Fountains Abbey when the life of cloisters proved too narrow for his immense spirit.

Tuck was a jovial mountain of a man, barrel-chested and broad-bellied, with a round face half-hidden by a bristling brown beard and a tonsured crown shining atop his head. His eyes were quick and merry, always one glance away from a wink. He wore a simple brown habit cinched at the waist with a rope, but beneath it he carried a broadsword and a buckler, and he knew how to use both. He was the only man in all of Sherwood Forest who could match Robin's wit as easily as his steel.

Their first meeting was a battle that became legend. Robin had heard rumors of a friar living near Fountains Abbey who could outfight any man in Yorkshire. Swearing an oath never to eat or drink until he found this holy terror, Robin set out with his bow and his pride. He found Tuck walking by a wild river, a stout cudgel in one hand and a sword at his hip, the very picture of a warrior monk. Robin, full of mischief, asked the friar to carry him across the water. Tuck, who knew mischief when he saw it, hoisted Robin onto his back without a word and waded into the swift current. But when they reached the far bank, Tuck drew his sword and demanded that Robin carry him back. The back-and-forth continued until, in the middle of the stream, Tuck flung Robin headlong into the water.

They fought with swords for six hours, neither gaining the advantage, their blades ringing through the forest. Robin blew his horn, and fifty bowmen appeared from the trees. Tuck put his fingers to his lips and whistled, and fifty ferocious bandogs bounded out of the forest, teeth bared. The dogs were trained to attack men, and they would have torn Robin's band apart. "Hold!" called Robin, laughing despite himself. "Stay your dogs, good friar, and let us be friends."

Tuck agreed, and from that day forward he was the Merry Men's chaplain. He heard their confessions, blessed their meals, and fought beside them in every skirmish. He was a man of profound contradictions: a holy man who drank ale from dawn till dusk, a peacemaker who wielded a blade as readily as a cross, a jester who could soothe the most troubled heart with a well-timed jest. He could say mass in the morning and crack a skull in the afternoon, and see no conflict between the two.

He was the one who married Robin and Maid Marian in the greenwood, beneath a canopy of oak leaves, with birds as witnesses and the wind as music. He baptized the children born in the forest and performed last rites for those who fell to the sheriff's men. He was, in many ways, the soul of Sherwood, proof that holiness and joy could be the same thing, that a man of God could also be a man of the people.

In the final days, when the Merry Men scattered, Tuck stayed on in the forest, living as a hermit. Some say he returned to his abbey and lived out his days in peace, a repentant sinner telling no one of his past. Others say he simply walked into the trees one morning and was never seen again. But wherever the story of Robin Hood is told, Friar Tuck is there, juggling, laughing, and showing the world that faith and joy belong together.

title: Finn, the Giant, and the Minster of Lund
summary: A giant named Finn builds Lund Cathedral in exchange for Saint Lawrence's eyes, but the saint overhears Finn's name in a lullaby and Finn turns to stone in the crypt. This world-famous master-builder legend is connected to several European cathedrals; Finn's petrified form can still be seen grasping a pillar in the crypt of Lund Cathedral.
full_text:
FINN, THE GIANT, AND THE MINSTER OF LUND
        IV
moral: Knowledge is power; a name held in secret can overcome even the strongest foe.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.III: Finn, the Giant, and the Minster of Lund. Retold by Dr. v. Sydow-Lund from variants in his collection. Connected to the world-famous giant master-builder legend, also associated with Nidaros Cathedral in Trondheim.

title: Faithful and Unfaithful
summary: Two brothers — one faithful, one unfaithful — set out into the world; the faithful brother wins a princess through kindness while the unfaithful one schemes and falls to ruin. This tale is a distant offshoot of the Tristan legend cycle.
moral: Faithfulness and kindness are always rewarded; treachery brings its own punishment.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.IX: Faithful and Unfaithful. From the Hyltén-Cavallius mss. collection. A distant offshoot of the Tristan legend cycle involving a golden-haired maiden and water spirits.

title: First Born, First Wed
summary: A king's son and a queen's daughter, promised to each other at birth, must overcome separation, enchantment, and a wicked stepmother before they can fulfill their destiny. This is a purely Swedish treatment of the redemption motif.
moral: True love promised at birth will find its way despite all obstacles.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XII: First Born, First Wed. From the mss. collection of Hyltén-Cavallius and Stephens, communicated by Dr. v. Sydow-Lund. A purely Swedish treatment of the redemption-motive.

title: Food for Kings: Oatcake and Herring
summary: When King Charles XI confiscated most noble properties in the Great Reduction of 1680, his aunt Countess Maria Eufrosyna of Höjentorp was affected. When the king came to visit, she greeted him with a box on the ear, then served him nothing but a herring's tail and an oatcake. When the king offered to return her property, she cut him off and gave him a second box on the ear. The king hastily commanded that she keep her property.
full_text: When King Charles XI of Sweden confiscated most noble properties for the crown in the Great Reduction of 1680, his aunt on his father's side, Countess Maria Eufrosyna of Höjentorp (daughter of John Casimir, sister of Charles X Gustav), was among those affected. Shortly after the confiscations, the king came to Höjentorp on one of his journeys. As he approached the entrance, the countess met him on the stairs and greeted him with a resounding box on the ear. Astounded, the king exclaimed, 'It is fortunate that it is me that you have struck! But why are you in such a combative mood, my aunt?' 'Why?' replied the countess. 'Because you have taken all my possessions from me.' She led the king to the dining hall and placed before him nothing but a herring's tail and an oatcake. 'Have you no better fare than this?' asked the king. 'No,' answered the lady, 'as you have spread the cloth, so you must dine.' When the king offered to return her gold and silver in exchange for a promise of generous provision until her death, she cut him off: 'Shame on you! Will you not allow me to keep so little as my gold and silver either?' Advancing on him, she gave him a second box on the ear. The king retreated hastily and commanded that the countess was to keep her property until the end of her days.
moral: A bold heart and quick wit can sometimes prevail against even the most powerful authorities.
source: Swedish folk tradition, collected by Herman Hofberg. Associated with Countess Maria Eufrosyna of Höjentorp (17th century).

title: Farmer Weathersky
summary: Farmer Weathersky possesses a unique ability to interact with the weather. When opportunistic neighbors try to exploit his gift or cheat him out of his land, he uses his cleverness to turn the tide in his favor, ensuring justice and prosperity.
full_text: Farmer Weathersky was a man who seemed to have the wind and rain in his pocket. While other farmers struggled with droughts or floods, Weathersky's fields were always perfectly watered and sunny. His success drew the jealousy of the local landlord and a few cunning neighbors, who attempted to trick him into a contract that would strip him of his land. Weathersky, however, was as sharp as a tack. He played along with their schemes, leading them to believe they had won, only to reveal a clause in the agreement that left the cheats bankrupt and himself with more land than ever. He lived out his days in peace, respected by all for his wit.
moral: Honesty and intelligence are the most reliable tools for protecting one's livelihood and defeating injustice.

title: Father Bruin in the Corner
summary: A humorous tale about Father Bruin, a bear who believes himself to be superior but is repeatedly tricked by the cunning Reynard the Fox. The story highlights the contrast between Bruin's strength and Reynard's intelligence.
full_text: Father Bruin was a bear of great size and even greater pride. He believed he was the king of the forest and that all other animals should defer to him. However, Reynard the Fox was far cleverer. Reynard often convinced Bruin to engage in 'tests of strength' or 'games of wit' that always ended with Bruin in a ridiculous position—often stuck in a corner or trapped in a hole. In one instance, Reynard convinced Bruin that the ice of a frozen lake was a fine place to fish, leading the bear to get his tail frozen fast. Through these mishaps, Bruin slowly learned that strength without wisdom is a fragile thing.
moral: Overconfidence and gullibility make one an easy target for those with superior wit.

title: Friends in Life and Death
summary: Two inseparable friends swear to remain loyal even after death. When one passes away, the other visits the grave and journeys into the afterlife. There, he observes the symbolic representations of human greed and contentment, learning a profound lesson about the soul.
full_text: Two young men swore they would never part, either in life or death. When one died, the survivor knocked on the grave, and the dead friend emerged. The survivor decided to accompany his friend into the afterlife. They passed through dark wastes and reached a land of green hills. They saw two herds of cattle: one herd was thin and wretched despite eating constantly, representing the greedy who are never satisfied. The other herd was fat and sleek despite having little to eat, representing the content. The survivor eventually returned to the living world, only to find that centuries had passed. Realizing the fleeting nature of time and the importance of peace, he accepted his fate with a quiet heart.
moral: True friendship transcends the boundary of death, and contentment is the only true wealth of the soul.

title: Faroese Chain Dance and Kvæði (Ballad Tradition)
summary: The Faroese chain dance (Føroya Dansur) accompanied by kvæði (ballads) is the national dance and a living tradition that preserves folklore. Dancers form a circle holding hands, moving rhythmically while a kvæðamaður (ballad singer) recites verses recounting tales of legendary heroes, folklore figures, and historical events. Many kvæði date to the Middle Ages and preserve stories otherwise lost, including the Sjúrðar Kvæði (cyclic ballads about Sigurd the Dragon-Slayer from Norse mythology) and ballads about Finnur hin Fróði and other Faroese figures. The tradition was instrumental in preserving the Faroese language during centuries of Danish rule and continues as a living cultural practice at festivals and gatherings.
full_text: The Faroese chain dance (Føroya Dansur) accompanied by kvæði (ballads) is the national dance and a living tradition that preserves folklore. Dancers form a circle holding hands, moving rhythmically while a kvæðamaður (ballad singer) recites verses recounting tales of legendary heroes, folklore figures, and historical events. Many kvæði date to the Middle Ages and preserve stories otherwise lost, including the Sjúrðar Kvæði (cyclic ballads about Sigurd the Dragon-Slayer from Norse mythology) and ballads about Finnur hin Fróði and other Faroese figures. The tradition was instrumental in preserving the Faroese language during centuries of Danish rule and continues as a living cultural practice at festivals and gatherings.
moral: Folklore preserves the wisdom of a people.
source: Wikipedia - Nordic Folklore; Trap.fo - Tradition and Tales on the Faroe Islands

title: Finnur hin Fróði and Faroese Wizard Legends
summary: Faroese folklore includes tales of powerful wizards and cunning folk (wise men and women) who could control weather, heal sickness, and ward off trolls. One of the most famous is Finnur hin Fróði (Finn the Wise), a legendary figure associated with magical knowledge and runic wisdom. These wizard legends blend Norse pagan traditions with later Christian elements—wizards were said to possess books of black magic (the 'Black Book' tradition) that could only be passed on to a willing successor. The island of Svínoy and the village of Sumba are particularly associated with wizard lore. These tales reflect the historical tension between official Christianity and older folk beliefs that persisted for centuries.
full_text:
Faroese folklore includes tales of powerful wizards and cunning folk (wise men and women) who could control weather, heal sickness, and ward off trolls. One of the most famous is Finnur hin Fróði (Finn the Wise), a legendary figure associated with magical knowledge and runic wisdom. These wizard legends blend Norse pagan traditions with later Christian elements—wizards were said to possess books of black magic (the 'Black Book' tradition) that could only be passed on to a willing successor. The island of Svínoy and the village of Sumba are particularly associated with wizard lore. These tales reflect the historical tension between official Christianity and older folk beliefs that persisted for centuries.

--- 
This story is preserved from an oral tradition. We acknowledge the communities and storytellers who have kept this legend alive through generations.
moral: Folklore preserves the wisdom of a people.
source: Superstition Sam - Witches and Wizards of the Faroe Islands; Faroese oral tradition

title: First Man and First Woman
summary: In the First World, Black Cloud and White Cloud came together in the East, and from their breath First Man (Áłtsé Hastiin) was formed, carrying white corn and crystal. Blue Cloud and Yellow Cloud came together in the West, forming First Woman (Áłtsé Asdzą́ą́) with yellow corn, white shell, turquoise, and yucca. First Woman made a fire with turquoise and First Man with crystal. Seeing each other's light, they joined together, establishing the pattern of life. Great Coyote (Mąʼiitoʼí Áłchíní) appeared, claiming to know all under water and sky. Then First Angry (Áłtsé Hashké) appeared, claiming seniority and bringing witchcraft into the world. Conflict among the Air-Spirit People led to their expulsion from the First World.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navajo Indians (1956); Wikipedia

title: Flat Pipe and the Arapaho Creation
summary: In the beginning, there was nothing but water covering everything. A man carrying a Flat Pipe walked on the water for six days, weeping and fasting, searching for a safe place to rest his pipe. On the seventh day, he called to the four directions and summoned seven cottonwood trees and all the creatures. One by one, various animals dove into the water to bring up mud—Grebe, waterfowls, Kingfisher, Otter, Beaver, Coot, Garter Snake, Black Snake, ducks, goose, crane—all failed. On the seventh dive, Turtle and the Man (who transformed his Flat Pipe into a Red-headed Duck) succeeded in bringing up mud. The Man cast the mud in four directions, creating the Earth.

title: Found-in-the-Grass
summary: Found-in-the-Grass is a rags-to-riches hero of Arapaho folklore. In some versions, he is an orphan abandoned as an infant and found by villagers in the tall grass. In others, he is the magical twin Spring-Boy grown older. Despite his humble beginnings, Found-in-the-Grass rises to become a great warrior and leader through courage, wit, and the help of spirit allies. He performs incredible feats, defeats enemies, and brings prosperity to his people. His story embodies the Arapaho value that one's origins do not determine one's destiny, and that perseverance and spiritual strength can overcome any disadvantage.

title: Finkingo: The Stickball Game (Choctaw)
summary: The Choctaw game of stickball, known as Finkingo or kabocha, is featured in many Choctaw legends. According to tradition, the game was given to the Choctaw by the spirits to resolve conflicts without warfare. Stickball is played with two small rackets and a ball, and it is extremely fast and violent. The game was often played between villages, with hundreds of players on each side, goals miles apart, and games lasting from sunrise to sunset. One legend tells of a young warrior who won the hand of a chief's daughter by scoring the winning goal in a legendary match. Another story describes how stickball settled a territorial dispute between the Choctaw and a neighboring tribe, avoiding a bloody war. The game remains central to Choctaw culture today.

title: Foam Woman and Ice Woman (Haida)
summary: Among the SGaana Kiidads (Supernatural Beings) of Haida tradition are Kalga Jaad (Ice Woman) and GaayhllGa Jaad (Flood-Tide-Woman). These female supernatural beings are part of the Raven Traveling narratives, also known as Xuuya Kaagang.ngas, which tell of Raven traveling the earth and transforming it to make it suitable for humans. Kalga Jaad controls ice and winter forces, while GaayhllGa Jaad governs the tides and the boundary between land and sea. These beings embody the Haida understanding of the interconnected relationship between land and sea. They are among the many supernatural entities that Raven encountered during his travels across Haida Gwaii, and their stories teach respect for natural forces and the balance required for human survival on the islands.
source: Terri-Lynn Williams-Davidson, Magical Beings of Haida Gwaii; Haida oral tradition

title: Fumo Liongo: The Warrior Poet of the Swahili Coast
summary: Liongo Fumo was a legendary Swahili prince, warrior, and poet who lived on Pate Island sometime between the 9th and 13th centuries. He was a giant of a man, nearly invulnerable except for his navel, and a master archer whose bow no other man could draw. Despite being the rightful heir, his brother Mringwari usurped the throne, forcing Liongo into exile. Liongo became a heroic outlaw, fighting against injustice and defending the Swahili people from Oromo invaders. He composed poems and songs that are still sung at weddings and gungu dances along the coast. His death came through betrayal — his own son was bribed to stab him in the navel with a copper needle. Mortally wounded, Liongo staggered to a well, dying upright with his bow in hand, his body so fearsome that no one dared approach until his mother confirmed his passing. His poetry remains the oldest surviving Swahili literature.
source: Swahili oral tradition, University of Cambridge research
moral: A true hero's legacy lives on through art and the hearts of the people.
